claims.claim_text: The exam environment may make nano available and may even default to it, depending on the current image.
claims.span_hint: line 43 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The exam environment may make nano available and may even default to it, depending on the current image.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The exam environment can vary, so be prepared to use more than one terminal editor safely.
claims.rationale: Editor availability and defaults are image-specific exam-environment details that are not pinned to a stable allowlisted public source in this form.

claims.claim_text: Deleting only the `image` line from the sample container leaves a manifest that Kubernetes will reject.
claims.span_hint: line 163 | section: How Vim Commands Map to YAML Structure
claims.anchor_text: In this manifest, deleting only `image: nginx:1.25` leaves a container with a name but no image, which Kubernetes rejects.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In this manifest, deleting only `image: nginx:1.25` removes a field the workload still needs, so deleting the whole container item is usually the safer structural edit.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes API reference marks `image` as optional, so the current sentence overstates guaranteed rejection while the structural-edit lesson can be preserved.

claims.claim_text: YAML indentation is semantic rather than cosmetic, and indentation changes can change structure even if a file still parses.
claims.span_hint: line 228 | section: Verify the Configuration Instead of Trusting It; line 778 | section: Did You Know
claims.rationale: This is a standards-level YAML behavior claim, and the authoritative YAML 1.2.2 specification is currently off the trusted-domain allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module ties this workflow to the Kubernetes 1.35 exam era.
claims.span_hint: line 285 | section: Create, Edit, and Validate a Manifest in a Tight Loop
claims.anchor_text: That quick client check is also a useful reminder that these examples target modern Kubernetes behavior, including the Kubernetes 1.35 exam era where `kubectl` dry-run and generated reference pages remain central to fast manifest work.
claims.suggested_rewrite: That quick client check is also a useful reminder that these examples target modern Kubernetes workflows, where `kubectl` dry-run and generated reference pages remain central to fast manifest work.
claims.rationale: The dated exam-version aside is unnecessary to the lesson and makes the sentence more brittle than the underlying workflow claim needs to be.

claims.claim_text: Pod labels belong under `metadata.labels`, not as a top-level field.
claims.span_hint: lines 348-349 | section: Recover from a Bad Indentation Edit
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes labels documentation explicitly shows labels attached in object metadata via `metadata.labels`.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl run ... --dry-run=client -o yaml` can generate a Pod manifest skeleton without creating the object.
claims.span_hint: line 376 | section: Editing Decision Matrix; lines 715-721 | section: Exam-Speed Editing Patterns
claims.rationale: The `kubectl run` reference explicitly documents client dry-run and YAML output for printing a generated object without creating it.

claims.claim_text: `containerPort` is an integer field in the Pod API, while `env.value` is a string field.
claims.span_hint: line 675 | section: Schema Error: Correct YAML, Wrong Field Shape
claims.rationale: The official Pod API reference explicitly types `ports.containerPort` as `int32` and `env.value` as `string`.

claims.claim_text: In a Deployment, Pod behavior depends on the Pod template labels and selector; changing only the Deployment object's top-level labels does not change which Pods its selector matches.
claims.span_hint: lines 703-707 | section: Intent Error: Valid Object, Wrong Location
claims.rationale: The Deployment documentation explicitly distinguishes `.metadata.labels` from `.spec.template.metadata.labels` and states that `.spec.selector` must match the Pod template labels.

claims.claim_text: A minimal-feature Vim is used by many Linux distributions as the default vi editor, and a small Vim is pre-installed on Mac and Linux.
claims.rationale: The official Vim repository README says `vim.tiny` is used by many Linux distributions as the default vi editor and that a small Vim is pre-installed on Mac and Linux.

claims.claim_text: `vimtutor` is an interactive practice tool installed with many vim packages.
claims.anchor_text: - **`vimtutor` is an interactive practice tool** installed with many vim packages. Running `vimtutor` for half an hour teaches movement, editing, undo, search, and save habits in a guided environment.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **`vimtutor` is an interactive practice tool** for learning core vim commands. Running `vimtutor` for half an hour can build movement, editing, undo, search, and save habits in a guided environment.
claims.rationale: The official Vim sources support `vimtutor` as a beginner training tool, but not the broader distribution-packaging claim in this exact form.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl apply --dry-run=client` previews the object locally without submitting it and can surface some validation issues before apply.
claims.span_hint: line 780 | section: Did You Know; lines 593-597 | section: Diagnose YAML Failures Like an Operator
claims.rationale: The `kubectl apply` reference documents client dry-run as printing the object without sending it, and its validation flags explain the validation behavior relevant to this workflow.

further_reading.title: kubectl apply Reference
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the authoritative reference for `--dry-run=client`, validation behavior, and manifest-application workflow.

further_reading.title: kubectl run Reference
further_reading.why_relevant: It shows how to generate Pod manifests quickly with client dry-run and YAML output.

further_reading.title: Vim Repository README
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the official starting point for Vim basics, including the built-in tutor and distribution notes.

notes: No existing citation pool was found for `k8s/cka/part0-environment`, so `section_pool_ref` is null. I skipped analogies, quiz answers, exercise checklists, tables, and code-block-only content per the task rules. The YAML-spec claim is real but needs allowlist expansion because the authoritative source is `yaml.org`.
